title: I. PanSeq library design
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"ges0","text":"Here, we discuss general principles of PhIP-Seq library design that served as a basis for the construction of PanSeq libraries. The protocol further provides an overview of tiling schemes, epitope and antigen representation, sources of antigens, and PepSyn commands that were used to generate PanSeq reference libraries.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"jf52","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e43as","text":"Library design files are available upon request.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: The underlying principle of PhIP-Seq library construction is that full-length protein sequences can be divided into peptides. These peptides, in turn, can be encoded by synthetic DNA oligonucleotides, cloned into linear T7 genomes, and expressed on the capsid of T7 bacteriophage for immunoprecipitation with biospecimens. The ability to fully encode a whole proteome in an oligonucleotide library is directly linked to advances in massively parallel oligo synthesis ( Kosuri & Church, 2014 ) and has greatly increased the power of phage display compared to earlier approaches, whereby representative cDNA libraries were needed. PhIP-Seq libraries rely on in-frame cloning of the peptide-encoding oligonucleotides 3'- to the 10B bacteriophage coat protein. Two primary factors restrict peptide length. First, the choice of T7 bacteriophage (phage) expression system has inherent tradeoffs that affect the performance of immunoprecipitations. Variable substitution of the 10A coat protein with 10B allows for the construction of low-, mid-, and high-copy display libraries that correspond to ~0.1, 5 - 15, and ~415 displayed peptides per phage, respectively. Accordingly, the low-, mid-, and high-copy systems can display peptides with practical maximum lengths of ~1200 amino acids (aa), ~120 aa, and ~50 aa, respectively. Low-copy systems allow for expression of full-length or nearly full-length proteins but do not facilitate fine epitope mapping. High-copy systems allow for the potential detection of high-avidity, low-affinity antibodies but are prone to toxicity, aggregation, and reduced library complexity. Mid-copy libraries offer a compromise between low- and high-copy libraries, yielding libraries of moderate complexity with some limited conformation epitope encoding ( Bartley et. al. 2023 ). The second constraint on peptide length is imparted by DNA oligonucleotide synthesis technology. Shorter oligos are cheaper, produced more efficiently, and have fewer errors, which effectively restricts the maximum oligo length and results in higher proportions of error-free oligos in shorter oligo pools. Given these factors, our PanSeq libraries are constructed from 230-nucleotide (nt) synthetic oligonucleotide library pools cloned into a mid-copy T7 phage display system. Each oligonucleotide includes 19 nt linkers on the 5’- and 3’- ends, and the remaining 192 nt encode 64 aa peptides.

PanSeq oligonucleotide pools were designed to be compatible with both phage display and in vitro mRNA display protocols, such as PepSeq ( Henson et al, 2022 ). PanSeq synthetic oligonucleotides differ from traditional PhIP-Seq oligonucleotide designs in that they lack 3’ termination codons and contain mRNA-display specific protein linker sequences at the 5’- and 3’- oligo ends. Thus, for T7 PhIP-Seq library cloning, PanSeq oligonucleotides must be extended by PCR amplification with primers that include termination codons and in-frame EcoRI/HindIII restriction sites (PCR step, Figure 1 ). The EcoRI/HindIII sites allow restriction and in-frame directional ligation into the T7 vector arms 3'- of the 10B gene. For sequencing library construction, we use similarly designed composite primers that convert common TEV/CP1 terminal sequences found in all tiles into adaptor sequences compatible with common Illumina-compatible indexing primer sets ( Figure 2 ). Figure 1. Schematic representation of cloning of individual peptide tiles into T7 vector arms to generate PanSeq libraries. RE digest = restriction enzyme digest. Figure 2 . Sequencing library construction and sequencing using PanSeq libraries.

To ensure representation of all potential linear epitopes, our PanSeq reference human proteome sublibrary is comprised of overlapping peptides. The length of the overlap exceeds linear epitope lengths, believed to be no longer than 10-12 aa in most cases ( Buus et al., 2012 ). Human PhIP-Seq designs commonly incorporate peptide overlaps of 14-25 aa, corresponding to 50% or less overlap. However, recent PhIP-Seq studies suggest that increased signal-to-noise can be achieved during data analysis by evaluating for co-enriched peptides that share epitopes ( Zamecnik et al. 2024 ; Bartley et. al. 2023 ). Therefore, to enhance the redundancy, reliability, and resolution of downstream analyses, we employed a 2/3 overlap peptide tile design, 64 aa peptide tiles that overlap by 42 aa ( Figure 2 ). Figure 2. Common peptide library tiling designs. 64/42 library design ensures at least 3x representation for the majority of sequence. Using this redundant design ensures 2x or higher representation for 98% of all 7-mers in reference proteome compared to ~80% for 64/20 design ( Figure 3 ). Figure 3. Coverage distribution of reference peptidome in 64/42 design vs 64/20 tiling design. >98% of all 7-mers are represented at least twice in 64/42 human proteome library. While the reference human peptidome was designed using a 64/42 tiling strategy, other designs were applied to other PanSeq sublibraries. Some parts of the collection (e.g., predicted splice variants) are unique in nature and are not tiled as a result. Other parts used a more efficient 64/20 design, and some of the exposure PanSeq sublibraries were reused from prior studies and thus utilized the original library design. All of the different tiling strategies used to generate parts of the PanSeq collection are summarized in Table 1 . Table 1. Peptide tiling design summary

The design goal of the PanSeq human library collection was to provide maximum coverage of all potential antigens encoded in the human genome ( Figure 4 ). Thus, in addition to comprehensive coverage of all well-annotated proteins – “reference proteome” ( H1 ) – we included those antigens that may be missed in standard annotations. The largest component of those additions is coding splice variants. All coding single intron retention and single exon skipping events were computationally predicted and included in the library design ( H2 ). The second addition to the reference proteome is OpenProt supplemental open reading frames (ORFs) ( H3 ). They include small ORFs 30-100 aa in size, ORFs encoded in transcripts annotated as non-coding RNAs (ncRNAs), and transcripts encoding multiple ORFs. Another relatively small component is comprised of coding sequences from retrotransposons. We included L1 proteins and all potential proteins encoded by Human Endogenous Retroviruses (HERVs) ( H4 ). We also incorporated positive and negative controls in the human PanSeq collection, including control oligos common with microbial library ( E2 component) and GFP peptide tiles (N=5). Figure 4. Summary of PanSeq human library collection design

H1: Human reference proteome: The human reference proteome design was based on GenCode v.44 release ( https://www.gencodegenes.org/human/release_44.html ). This version of human genome annotation included N=19,396 protein-coding genes, and a total number of 89,067 protein-coding transcripts. All peptide manipulation steps were performed using pepsyn v.0.4.1 ( https://github.com/lasersonlab/pepsyn ), and amino-acid sequence clustering was performed using CD-HIT following the general outline presented in Mohan et al., 2018 . Translations for all annotated coding sequences (excluding c-terminal peptides) were downloaded and broken into peptide tiles using pepsyn as follows: zcat gencode.v44.pc_translations.fa.gz \ | pepsyn x2ggsg - - \ | pepsyn tile -l 64 -p 42 - - \ | pepsyn disambiguateaa - - \ > gencode44.w64s42.raw.tiles.fasta Peptide tiles for the reference set of C-terminal peptides are prepared separately: zcat gencode.v44.pc_translations.fa.gz \ | pepsyn x2ggsg - - \ | pepsyn ctermpep -l 64 - - \ | pepsyn disambiguateaa - - \ > gencode44.w64.cterm.raw.tiles.fasta Obtained peptide tiles were clustered using CD-HIT to remove redundancy: cd-hit -i gencode44.w64s42.raw.tiles.fasta \ -o gencode44.w64s42.cdhit53.tiles.fasta \ -c 0.95 -G 0 -A 53 -M 0 -T 1 -d 0 And we repeat the same process for C-terminal peptides: cd-hit -i gencode44.w64.cterm.raw.tiles.fasta \ -o gencode44.w64.cterm.cdhit.tiles.fasta \ -c 0.95 -G 0 -aL 1.0 -aS 1.0 -M 0 -T 1 -d 0 As a result, we obtained N = 516,435 de-duplicated internal peptide tiles and N = 69,242 de-duplicated C-terminal peptide tiles, for a total of N = 585,677 peptides that comprise the human reference proteome. These peptide sequences were merged with other parts of the collection ( H2 - H4 ) and used in the final step to generate nucleotide encodings.

The exposure component of the TIU PanSeq library was developed to evaluate immune responses to a diverse range of microbial pathogens, including bacteria, viruses, fungi, and a limited set of allergens. To improve data reuse and the ability to cross-reference our data with previously published datasets, we adapted two previously published library designs, VirScan2 ( E1 ) ( Schubert et. al., 2019 ) and human microbiome ( E2 )( Vogl et al., 2021 ), to the PanSeq format. Additionally, a third pathogen library, ( E3 ), was derived de novo from the antigens in the Immune Epitope Database ( IEDB ). While some microbial antigens from IEDB are also present in the E2 library, the E3 library includes fungal, parasitic, and allergen antigens. Figure 5. Summary of PanSeq exposure library collection design

E1: VirScan2 viral antigens The viral exposure component of PanSeq collection was based on the previously published VirScan2 design ( Schubert et. al., 2019 ). This pan-viral collection was designed to provide a comprehensive representation of vertebrate, mosquito-borne, and tick-borne viral genomes. Because VirScan2 was designed using 62 aa peptides, the peptide sequences were padded to 64 aa to ensure seamless compatibility with other PanSeq collection libraries. Since VirScan2 design precedes the discovery of SARS-CoV-2, we supplemented VirScan2 with SARS-CoV-2 peptides. The proteomes of the major SARS-CoV-2 strains starting with the reference genome and up to XBB.1.5, BA.2.12.1, and EG5.1 were downloaded from NextStrain on 08/09/2023. These proteomes were split into 64 aa peptides with 20 aa overlap ( Table 1 ) and de-duplicated as before. As a result, N=510 peptides were added to VirScan2 library. Subsequently, nucleotide encodings were generated and the oligonucleotide sequences were supplemented with the same GFP-derived control oligos (N=5) as were used in the human PanSeq library. cat VirScan2.merged.pept.fasta \ | pepsyn stripstop - - \ | pepsyn pad -l 64 - - \ | pepsyn disambiguateaa - - \ | pepsyn revtrans --codon-freq-threshold 0.01 - - \ | pepsyn prefix -p CCTATACTTCCAAGGCGCA - - \ | pepsyn suffix -s GGTGACTCTCTGTCTTGGC - - \ | pepsyn recodesite--site EcoRI --site HindIII --site XhoI --clip-left 19 --clip-right 19

E2: Microbiome/bacterial antigens TIU pool P7 uses a previously published microbial library PhIP-Seq design ( Vogl et al., 2021 ). For antigenic inclusion in the library, priority was given to abundant genes and species detected in microbiome samples that were likely immunogenic, proteins predicted to be secreted or membrane-associated. It was subsequently supplemented with known or likely immunogenic proteins from pathogenic, probiotic, and antibody-coated bacterial species and virulence factors. In total, the microbiome/bacterial library consists of 244,000 64 aa peptides from 28,668 different proteins. This peptide set is comprised of peptides from metagenome-assembled genomes (N=147,061), pathogenic bacteria (N=61,250), bacteria known to be coated with antibodies (N=22,050), probiotic bacteria (N=14,700), virulence factors from the virulence factor database ( VFDB ) (N=24,164), and control peptides (N=11,525). The set of 11,525 control peptides consists of two major classes derived from the IEDB database: positive and negative controls in the context of infectious diseases. Positive controls include all antigens from B-cell assays annotated as infectious diseases with human host and excluding parasites (N=4,250), and as negative controls, antigens from human autoimmune diseases were included (N=7,700) ( Vogl et al., 2021 ). Additionally, this library includes N=759 technical controls. They include N=100 oligos encoding random amino-acid sequences; N=364 negative controls: abundant human proteins that are uncommonly auto-reactive, such as histones, serum albumin, and proteins that should not have been encountered by most healthy individuals (e.g., Ebola proteins). The remaining N=295 peptides are from viral proteins that were shown to elicit frequent antibody responses in 47.9%-97.2% of humans and were detected by PhIP-Seq ( Xu et al., 2015 ). This set of control peptides was also included in the P4 human pool and E3 library. PanSeq-compatible oligos were generated from the amino acid sequences of the above peptides without modifications to the peptide sequence.

E3: IEDB antigens: All reported antigenic targets of human B cells, excluding human, mouse, and rat proteins, were downloaded from IEDB on 07/14/2023 (N=1,695 proteins). Full-length amino-acid sequences of these proteins were split into 64/20 aa peptide tiles, de-duplicated as before, and used to generate oligonucleotide sequences. This IEDB ( P9 TIU) pool was supplemented with pos/neg/rnd control sequences from the exposure library, E2 (N=759) and N=5 GFP peptides. Ultimately, the IEDB library comprises N=32,131 peptides that contain the full set of controls (N=764, E2 controls + GFP peptides) and therefore can be used alone or together with other elements of the PanSeq collection.

H2: Coding splice variants: Although many alternatively spliced transcriptional isoforms are included in Gencode annotation, some are inevitably missed due to the lack of high-coverage transcriptome data and diversity of tissue- and cell-type specific alternative splicing. These alternatively spliced isoforms represent an important source of protein diversity ( Goldtzvik et al., 2023 ) and can be immunoreactive. While many types of alternative splicing require experimental data for prediction (e.g., alternative exon inclusion or alternative 5'/3' UTR), two classes of the most common alternative splicing events - single exon skipping and intron retention - re-use existing exon-intron boundaries (see, for example, Zhang et al., 2021 ) and can be predicted from existing genome annotations. We thus predicted all of the potential coding intron retention and single exon skipping events for all coding isoforms of Gencode v.44 genes. Since we are interested in novel potential epitopes, sequences where a stop codon was introduced immediately following an exon boundary were excluded. We also required at least 3 non-reference amino acids in each peptide. All of these peptides were truncated at a maximum length of 64 aa (our tile length) and de-duplicated using cd-hit as was done for the reference proteome ( H1 ). This resulted in N = 304,531 peptides corresponding to potential novel splicing isoforms that are not included in current reference transcriptomes.

H3: OpenProt supplemental ORFs: Human OpenProt release 1.6 was downloaded from the OpenProt website ( https://www.openprot.org/downloads , currently available at https://archive.openprot.org/ ). For the analysis, we used only those ORFs that were supported by either mass-spec or ribosome profiling experimental data. These ORFs were split into 64/20 peptide tiles and de-duplicated using cd-hit: cat uORF.sorted.fasta \ | pepsyn x2ggsg - - \ | pepsyn tile -l 64 -p 20 - - \ | pepsyn disambiguateaa - - \ > uORF.w64s20.raw.tiles.fasta cat uORF.sorted.fasta \ | pepsyn x2ggsg - - \ | pepsyn ctermpep -l 64 - - \ | pepsyn disambiguateaa - - \ > uORF.w64.cterm.raw.tiles.fasta cd-hit -i uORF.w64.cterm.raw.tiles.fasta \ -o uORF.w64.cterm.cdhit.tiles.fasta \ -c 0.95 -G 0 -aL 1.0 -aS 1.0 -M 0 -T 1 -d 0 cd-hit -i uORF.w64s20.raw.tiles.fasta \ -o uORF.w64s20.cdhit53.tiles.fasta \ -c 0.95 -G 0 -A 53 -M 0 -T 1 -d 0 Following these steps, we obtained N=51,122 peptides that were used to generate nucleotide encodings.

H4: HERV and LINE retroelements: All sequences annotated with the keyword “HERV” or “LINE-1” of human origin were downloaded from UniProt on 08/02/2023. HERV and LINE-1 elements correspond to human endogenous retroviruses (HERV-K, HERV-H, etc.) and long interspersed nuclear element-1, respectively. HERV and LINE-1 related protein sequences were split into 64/42 peptide tiles and de-duplicated in the same way as human reference proteome (see Table 1 ). As a result, we obtained N = 2,582 HERV and LINE-1 derived sequences. Most of the sequences in this set are HERV-associated.

Generating final nucleotide encoding: Following the generation of peptide sets H1 - H4 , they were merged into a single fasta file (N=942,621 peptides) and used to generate final nucleotide encodings using pepsyn: cat hg.merged.pept.fasta \ | pepsyn stripstop - - \ | pepsyn pad -l 64 - - \ | pepsyn disambiguateaa - - \ | pepsyn revtrans --codon-freq-threshold 0.01 - - \ | pepsyn prefix -p CCTATACTTCCAAGGCGCA - - \ | pepsyn suffix -s GGTGACTCTCTGTCTTGGC - - \ | pepsyn recodesite --site EcoRI --site HindIII --site XhoI --clip-left 19 --clip-right 19 - - >Gencode44_plus.TIU_2023.na.fasta For oligonucleotide synthesis, the merged fasta file with nucleotide encodings was split into 4 parts of 244,000 sequences (oligonucleotide pools P1 - P4 , pool P4 contains N=210,621 sequences): split -l 244000 Gencode44_plus.TIU_2023.na.csv Gencode44_plus.TIU_2023. The final oligonucleotide pool ( P4 ) was supplemented with pos/neg/rnd (rnd = random) control sequences from the exposure library, E2 (N=759, see below for detailed description), and N=5 GFP peptides. As a result, the total number of individual oligonucleotides in the PanSeq human library is N=943,385. The four 244,000 oligonucleotide pools ( P1 - P4 ) were submitted to Agilent for massively parallel synthesis.
creator: Thomas Esparza
